coredata.dc:title: Designing for Manufacturing Scalability in Clean Energy Research
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Kevin J.
Li, Liang
Olivetti, Elsa A.
coredata.dc:description: Kevin Huang is a postdoctoral associate in the Department of Materials Science and Engineering at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT). He studies methods for assessing and enhancing the manufacturing scalability of laboratory-derived clean energy technologies. He has a PhD from MIT, an MS from the University of Illinois, Urbana-Champaign, and a BS from Cornell University, all in materials science and engineering. Liang Li is a graduate student in the Technology and Policy Program at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. Her current research is focused on the manufacturing scalability of materials for clean energy applications. She holds a bachelor's degree in chemical engineering from the National University of Singapore. Elsa Olivetti is the Atlantic Richfield Associate Professor of Energy Studies in the Department of Materials Science and Engineering at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. Her research focuses on improving the environmental and economic sustainability of materials using methods informed by materials economics, machine learning, experimental inquiry, and techno-economic analysis. Dr. Olivetti received her BS degree in engineering science from the University of Virginia. Her PhD in materials science and engineering from MIT focused on the development of cathode materials for lithium ion batteries.

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This article is made available under the Elsevier license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mass Transport in Common PV Thin Film Deposition Processes

In spin coating, a liquid-phase solution is placed on a substrate, which is then rotated at high speed to eject excess fluid and solvent, leaving a thin solid film behind. Spray casting, while also liquid phase, instead relies on the pressurized delivery of solution through a nozzle, after which the solid flow breaks into successively smaller droplets until depositing on the substrate. Vapor deposition methods form thin solid films through gas-phase delivery and subsequent nucleation and growth of atomic and molecular species at the substrate.
Key Differences between Production in Lab and Manufacturing at Scale

As the goal in lab is typically to maximize the performance of a produced technology, the set of key process drivers is commensurately narrow. Manufacturing at scale, however, necessitates the simultaneous optimization of product performance, production reproducibility, production volume, and production cost. Accordingly, the process factors that drive production at scale are more numerous and varied. By considering some, or all, of these factors in lab, researchers may be able to design technologies that are inherently scalable and manufacturable.

Often a prevailing driver of production cost, the yield of complete manufacturing process flows rapidly diminishes with an increase in the number of process steps. This is intensified by even modest decreases in the yield of individual unit operations.

simple-head.ce:title: Designing for Manufacturing Scalability in Clean Energy Research

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technology and Policy Program, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Kevin Huang is a postdoctoral associate in the Department of Materials Science and Engineering at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT). He studies methods for assessing and enhancing the manufacturing scalability of laboratory-derived clean energy technologies. He has a PhD from MIT, an MS from the University of Illinois, Urbana-Champaign, and a BS from Cornell University, all in materials science and engineering.

Liang Li is a graduate student in the Technology and Policy Program at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. Her current research is focused on the manufacturing scalability of materials for clean energy applications. She holds a bachelor's degree in chemical engineering from the National University of Singapore.

Elsa Olivetti is the Atlantic Richfield Associate Professor of Energy Studies in the Department of Materials Science and Engineering at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. Her research focuses on improving the environmental and economic sustainability of materials using methods informed by materials economics, machine learning, experimental inquiry, and techno-economic analysis. Dr. Olivetti received her BS degree in engineering science from the University of Virginia. Her PhD in materials science and engineering from MIT focused on the development of cathode materials for lithium ion batteries.

To fully realize the potential of innovative new clean energy technologies, they must first be produced at large scale and low cost. The scalability of such technologies, however, is driven by the constraints of their large-scale manufacturing processes. By considering how the materials properties, process infrastructure, and production cost of their technologies scale, researchers may be able to design clean energy technologies that are not only high performing but also highly scalable and manufacturable.

ce:section.ce:para: To forestall the most destructive and irreversible effects of global climate change, we have a mere 30 years to increase our energy supply's share of low- and zero-carbon energy by more than 300% relative to that in 2010. 1 History has shown, however, that the clean energy technologies required to effect such sweeping changes typically require many decades to scale up and deploy. 2 Lithium ion battery cathodes, for example, were originally demonstrated in their modern form in 1980 at Oxford. 3 It was not until 2008, however, that they were first used in production automobiles. 4 Were it to take a similar length of time to deploy today's clean energy innovations, we will likely fail to avert the more devastating effects of climate change. We must, therefore, accelerate the speed with which materials-dependent clean energy technologies are scaled up.

Promising ideas continue to emerge from research labs around the world. To fully realize the impact of these potentially transformative technologies, however, we must first manufacture them at large scale and low cost. Encouragingly, researchers have begun to identify some of the key factors that could hinder or accelerate the transition to manufacturing. Among these are considerations such as materials supply, materials cost, and market alignment. 5,6

In this Commentary, we illustrate how the scalability of new clean energy technologies is driven by the constraints of their large-scale manufacturing processes. In particular, we discuss three determinants of manufacturing scalability: (1) the translation of lab-scale materials properties to manufacturing-scale processes, (2) the supply and supporting infrastructure of the equipment on which those processes rely, and (3) the unit production costs at scale for complete manufacturing process flows. By assessing these factors early on, researchers may be able to design technologies that are not only high performing but also highly manufacturable.

To varying degrees, production-scale processes differ from their lab-scale counterparts. Methods used in lab sometimes do not even have direct equivalents at scale. As a result, when transitioning to higher production volumes or larger product scales, it can be challenging to consistently produce the same heat and mass transport environments required to obtain the desired materials properties. Successful reproduction of these synthesis or reaction conditions at scale may be governed by process factors that are not considered in lab.

Awareness of these disparities presents an opportunity for researchers. By understanding the characteristics of production processes and equipment and how they diverge from those used in lab, researchers may be able to anticipate how the desired materials properties, and the process factors that drive them, will change upon scale up. In effect, by studying the attributes, constraints, and practical limitations of large-scale processes, researchers may learn how to control the heat and mass transport conditions necessary to realize the desired properties at scale.

BP Solar, for example, attempted to scale a CdTe photovoltaic (PV) technology. 7 Much of the original R&D process was manual. The precursor solutions were mixed by hand and small area substrates were manually inserted for deposition. At production, these steps were mechanically controlled to accommodate the larger product scale. While the two processes were similar, regulation of the proper thermal and mass transfer profiles inside the production reactor was so difficult to repeatedly achieve that large-scale batches exhibited both deficient film uniformity as well as significant batch-to-batch variability. This is perhaps not surprising. As stirred tank reactors increase in size, the variations in local solution velocity become more pronounced, resulting in uneven reaction profiles and, as a consequence, inconsistent product properties at scale. 8

However, when large-scale processes—and, correspondingly, the heat and mass transport conditions they produce—are sufficiently understood and can be successfully controlled, the prescribed materials specifications may be translated across production scales. 9,10 Functional polymer thin films, for example, may be deposited through a number of chemical vapor deposition techniques. By judiciously managing the reaction environment so that the balance between convective and diffusive precursor transport remains invariant across the processes of interest, films of commensurate properties may be obtained through a variety of methods—whether batch or continuous, small or large scale—even when the nominal process parameters, such as absolute reactor pressure, differ.

Translating the same materials properties across even more divergent processes may not be as straightforward. For example, to fabricate laboratory PVs, many researchers rely on small, quick, low-cost solution methods such as spin coating, for which no large-scale equivalent exists. As a result, to scale up, a different process must be employed—spray or vapor deposition, for instance. As schematically illustrated in Figure 1 , spin coating is driven by the rotational velocity of a viscous fluid, spray coating by the pressurized, turbulent flow of such fluids, and vapor deposition by atomic and molecular gas-phase transport. Given these disparities, understanding gained from one method may not readily translate to another. If researchers were to, instead, employ lab-scale analogs of widely used large-scale processes, lessons learned in lab about key process factors could then transfer more easily to production. For example, in using lab-scale vapor deposition reactors to fabricate perovskite PVs, researchers can obtain both high-performance devices and the process insight necessary to accelerate scale up. 11 Ultimately, by understanding both lab-scale processes and equipment as well as those that must be used at scale, researchers can maximize the potential for knowledge transfer upon scale up.

In the same way that the wide deployment of clean energy technologies requires abundant, low-cost raw materials and precursors, so too does it require widely available process equipment and the auxiliary infrastructure to support it. For example, equipment that is broadly commercially available through a variety of suppliers is less likely to inhibit scale up than equipment that must be procured on an aftermarket, or, worse yet, equipment that must be custom fabricated. In addition, scalable processes need both the corresponding intellectual capital to expertly manage them and ecosystems that ensure sustained operation and maintenance.

BP Solar, when attempting to scale their CdTe PVs, primarily used commercially available process equipment for the majority of their unit operations. The wet reactors to electrochemically deposit CdS and CdTe, however, had to be custom fabricated. Unfortunately, few fabricators existed with both the large-scale expertise and the necessary geographic proximity. The difficulty of manufacturing these reactors significantly delayed their delivery, resulting in cost increases in excess of 100%. Installing the reactors was, likewise, unexpectedly difficult and time consuming. And owing to the uniqueness of the process and the equipment, BP struggled to acquire the requisite workforce talent. Ultimately, capital expenditures escalated far beyond budget and production timelines were irreversibly delayed. 7

Silicon PV technology, by contrast, relies on processes that have been largely borrowed from the semiconductor industry. As a result, the necessary equipment is widely available, the production of that equipment has long since captured economies of scale, replacement parts are easy to acquire, and the critical operating and maintenance expertise, knowledge, and heuristics for that equipment are widespread and easily transferrable. Thus, by employing processes for which the equipment and supporting infrastructure are prevalent, researchers may be able to ensure a degree of scalability in their technologies.

The synthesis of complex nanostructured materials can often proceed by numerous pathways. For example, to obtain doped silicon nanowires of uniform size and carrier density, researchers may (1) synthesize, assemble, and filter as-doped Si nanoparticles, (2) synthesize, dope, and filter pure Si nanowires, or (3) synthesize and filter as-doped Si nanowires directly. It is often not intuitively clear, however, which of these pathways is most scalable. 12 Process factors that are immaterial in lab can have considerable cost implications at scale. This is especially true given that the desired outcomes at scale include objectives beyond materials performance (see Figure 2 ). As such, even if the specified materials properties can be consistently obtained at production, the cost of doing so may not scale as effectively. Therefore, by evaluating the unit production costs, and their principal determinants, at scale, researchers may be able to chart the most economically advantageous pathway to scale up.

As an example, consider the use of batch-style hydro- or solvothermal processes in nanomaterials synthesis. 13 In lab, such methods are low cost, quick to learn, and allow for flexible prototyping of a wide range of materials. At scale, however, their cost feasibility can become driven by factors that are not considered or not prohibitive in lab. For example, given the necessary high temperature, high pressure conditions, the size (and hence, cost) of the reactors can rapidly escalate with batch process scale. This, in turn, coincides with a decrease in product yield, a consequence of the reaction profile's increased variability within the chamber. 8,13 Materials costs, as a result, are exacerbated due to waste. Likewise, the long cycle times (reaction times as high as 12–24 hr) for batch hydrothermal processes can be made even worse at scale by the protracted times required to ramp large reactors up to (and cool down from) the target reaction temperatures. These collective effects can raise materials, energy, and capital expenditures. Compounding this, the low yield and long cycle times result in poor throughput. Taken together, the high costs and low production volumes of large-scale, batch-style processes can, in some cases, lead to prohibitively high unit production costs. 14

However, many of the same process factors that might prevent batch hydrothermal processes from economically scaling could rationalize the scale up of a continuous hydrothermal process. 13,14 Confining the reaction volume to a small, but continuous flow, region affords more precise control over heat and mass transfer, thereby generating a more consistent, higher yield, and lower waste product. As an added benefit, scaled flow reactors need not be significantly larger than their lab-scale counterparts. 13 Furthermore, while batch reactors require at least several hours of reaction time for particle nucleation and growth, continuous flow processes only require a few seconds or minutes. These differences can result in lower equipment, energy, and materials costs, while the higher production rate and process yield boost overall throughput. Accordingly, unit production costs at scale can sometimes favor a continuous flow process over its batch-style equivalent. 14

While assessing the production costs of individual unit operations can aid in identifying techniques that are suitable for large-scale production, 12 the scalability of nanomaterials manufacture must ultimately be evaluated in the context of its entire manufacturing process flow. For instance, to obtain macroscale products from complex nanostructured building blocks, numerous synthesis operations are typically required. Whether or not individually cost effective, these must often be accompanied by separations operations to isolate the desired outputs and assembly operations to hierarchically construct the final products. 12 This adds time, capital expenditures, and materials loss to the overall process. What’s worse, these effects are multiplicative. Even when the yield of each step in a process is high, say 95%, the cumulative yield of the entire process flow (i.e., 0.95 n , where n is the number of steps) can, in some cases, be considerably lower. 8,12 As illustrated in Figure 3 , even relatively simple processes (e.g., those with fewer than ten discrete steps) can exhibit low overall process yield. As a result, excessively elaborate, many-step manufacturing processes can become hampered by untenable economics at production, regardless of their attractiveness in lab.

However, by evaluating the production costs (and principal cost drivers) of complete manufacturing process flows at scale, researchers may be able to identify process options in lab that are more intrinsically scalable. Moreover, when such analyses are integrated with thoughtful consideration of how materials properties and process infrastructure scale as well, a holistic approach to scalable technology design is possible in clean energy research.

Many of the concerns discussed in this Commentary are not currently regarded by researchers. As Walton and Sholl point out, these considerations are too frequently assumed to be someone else's burden. 15 As a result, when lab-derived technologies are scaled up, disconnects between the design of the technology and the constraints of the manufacturing process can force long and expensive redesigns. 16 Instead, by learning from process engineers, researchers may be able to design clean energy technologies that are innately scalable, a considerable advantage in an otherwise crowded and competitive research space. To that end, we present two specific recommendations to supplement the principles already discussed.

First, researchers should endeavor to understand how the heat and mass transport conditions they require will evolve at higher production volumes and larger product scales. Surrogate lab-scale experiments that simulate the synthesis and reaction environments in large-scale processes should be conducted as a means of understanding how process factors at scale—particularly factors that are not typically considered in lab—will impact the translation of lab-scale materials properties. For example, while it may be impractical to use a commercial-scale, laminar flow oven in lab, the effect of the spatial and temporal variance in the temperature profile on large-scale batches could be reproduced in a lab-scale furnace through individual, manual experiments at different time and temperature points. This recommendation complements an earlier suggestion by Walton and Sholl to approximate the deployment environment of new materials with proxy chemical mixtures. 15 Second, researchers should embrace the multitude of tools that already exists to evaluate manufacturing process cost. 6 Understanding the large-scale economics of production manufacturing can enable researchers to identify the most promising and scalable options from the range of available process pathways in the lab.

To meet the clear and immediate challenges posed by global climate change, the research community continues to develop ever more promising solutions. We are gated, however, by our ability to rapidly scale these ideas from lab to production. Therefore, by empowering researchers to examine critical manufacturing process factors in the lab, when it is least costly but most impactful to do so, 16 we may be able to design and deploy the inherently scalable clean energy technologies that we so urgently need.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge useful input from anonymous reviews and discussions with K. Gleason. This work was supported, in part, by a seed grant from the MIT Energy Initiative Low Carbon Energy Center on Storage and received partial support from the National Science Foundation Award #1534340, DMREF and the Office of Naval Research under Contract No. N00014-16-1-2432.
